title: Speculative Decoding
lesson:
Speculative decoding is a trick to make a big language model spit out text faster without changing a single word it would have said. Read that again: same output, same quality, just quicker. It sounds like cheating, but it is built on a clever piece of maths. To get it, you first need to understand the slow part of running a model.

Why generation is slow

When a model writes text, it does it one token (a token is a word-chunk, like "cat" or "-ing") at a time. To produce token number 50, it must first have produced tokens 1 through 49. This is autoregressive generation — "auto" (self) plus "regressive" (looking back) — meaning each new token feeds back in to help write the next one. So the model can't sprint ahead; it shuffles forward one step per step.

Here is the painful detail. Each step does a forward pass — one full trip of the input through every layer of the giant model. For a large model that trip moves billions of weights from VRAM (the GPU's fast memory) into the chips that do the maths. The bottleneck is almost never the maths itself; it is shovelling all those weights across the memory bus. And here is the insult: doing a forward pass for one token costs nearly the same as doing it for twenty tokens, because you paid the big cost (loading the weights) either way. The GPU is mostly sitting idle, waiting on memory. That waste is the door speculative decoding walks through.

The draft-then-check idea

Picture a slow, careful author (the big model) and a fast, eager intern (a small "draft" model). The intern reads the story so far and scribbles a guess at the next several words. Then the author glances at the whole guess in one read and either nods ("yes, that's exactly what I would have written") or crosses out from the first wrong word onward and writes the correct one. The intern is sometimes wrong, but checking a finished guess is far cheaper than writing from scratch.

That is speculative decoding. A small, cheap draft model proposes, say, 4 tokens ahead. The big target model then runs one forward pass over all 4 proposed tokens at once. Remember: one pass over 4 tokens costs about the same as one pass over 1 token. So in a single expensive step, the big model can confirm up to 4 tokens instead of 1. If the intern guessed well, you just went 4x faster for free.

A second analogy: predictive text on your phone. As you type, it suggests "...see you tomorrow." If the suggestion matches what you wanted, you tap it and skip ahead several letters. If not, you ignore it and type normally. You never accept a word you didn't mean — you only save time when the guess happens to be right.

Why the output stays identical

The magic is the verification rule. The target model checks each drafted token against its own probabilities. If the draft token is one the target would plausibly have chosen, it is accepted; the moment a token fails the test, everything after it is thrown away and the target generates that one token itself. A bit of probability maths (called rejection sampling) guarantees the final stream of tokens has the exact same statistical distribution as if the big model had written every token alone. So speculative decoding is lossless — it changes speed, never quality. This is different from quantization, which trades a little accuracy for speed.

What it looks like in practice

Modern serving engines like vLLM turn this on with a flag. You pair a big model with a small one from the same family (so they speak the same token language):

vllm serve meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B \
  --speculative-config '{"method":"draft_model",
    "model":"meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B",
    "num_speculative_tokens":5}'

You can even skip the second model entirely. A method called n-gram speculation guesses the next tokens by copying repeated phrases from the text already on screen — great for code or summaries that echo their input. Newer models also ship with built-in multi-token prediction heads (small extra layers that predict several tokens at once), which act as a free draft model baked into the target. The key number to watch is the acceptance rate: the fraction of drafted tokens the target keeps.

Plain decodingSpeculative decoding
1 token per big-model passUp to N tokens per big-model pass
GPU often idle, waiting on memoryUses the idle capacity to verify a batch
Speed fixed by model sizeSpeed depends on draft acceptance rate
One model to loadTwo models (or a built-in draft head)

💡 Speculative decoding shines for single-user, latency-sensitive chat. Under heavy batch load the GPU is already busy, so the free idle capacity vanishes and the speedup shrinks.
mentalModel: A slow, careful author with a fast intern. The intern scribbles the next few words; the author proofreads the whole guess in one glance, keeping the right part and rewriting from the first mistake. Right guesses save time; wrong ones cost nothing extra. Same final story, written faster.
mistakes: Thinking it lowers quality. It does not. The verification maths makes the output statistically identical to the big model alone. If quality changed, the implementation is buggy, not the method.
Using a draft model with a different tokenizer. The draft and target must share the same token vocabulary, or their guesses can't be compared. Pick the draft from the same model family.

Picking a draft model that is too big or too small. Too big and drafting itself gets slow; too small and its guesses get rejected constantly (low acceptance rate). A draft roughly 10x smaller than the target is a common sweet spot — but measure, don't assume.

Expecting the same speedup under heavy load. The free lunch comes from idle GPU capacity. With large batches the GPU is already saturated, so gains drop sharply. It is a single-user, low-latency win first.

Confusing it with parallel generation of different tokens. The model still produces tokens in order. It just verifies several candidate next tokens in one pass; it does not write paragraph 3 before paragraph 2.

Ignoring acceptance rate when tuning. If you set num_speculative_tokens too high, most drafts past the first few get rejected and you waste draft compute. Watch the acceptance rate and tune the draft length to match it.
exercise:
Using vLLM (or any engine that supports it), serve a mid-size open model twice: once normally, and once with a small draft model from the same family (in vLLM, pass --speculative-config with num_speculative_tokens set to 4). Send the same prompt to both and measure tokens-per-second. Try one prompt that is highly predictable (e.g. "Repeat the alphabet: a, b, c, ...") and one that is open-ended (e.g. "Invent a surprising metaphor for time"). Compare the speedups.

Stretch goal: Find and log the acceptance rate for each prompt. Explain in two sentences why the predictable prompt is accepted more often, and estimate the theoretical maximum speedup from that acceptance rate.
solution: Hint: You should see a clear speedup with speculation on, and a bigger one for the predictable prompt because the draft model guesses repetitive text almost perfectly, so its acceptance rate is high (often 80%+). The open-ended prompt is harder to guess, so more drafts get rejected and the speedup is smaller. Roughly, if the average accepted run length is k tokens per big-model pass, your speedup ceiling is about kx — minus the small overhead of running the draft model each step. So a 90% acceptance rate over 4 drafted tokens might yield a real-world 2–3x, not the naive 4x, once draft cost is counted.
summary: Speculative decoding speeds up generation by letting a small draft model guess several tokens ahead, then having the big target model verify them all in one forward pass (which costs almost the same as verifying one). A rejection-sampling rule keeps the output mathematically identical to the big model alone, so it is lossless. The speedup depends on the acceptance rate and shines most for single-user, low-latency chat rather than heavy batched serving.

keyTerms.def: Generating text faster by drafting several tokens with a small model and verifying them with the big model in one pass, with no change to the final output.
Writing one token at a time, where each new token depends on all the tokens before it. This is why plain generation can't skip ahead.
One full trip of the input through every layer of the model to compute the next token. It is the expensive step being optimized.
A small, fast model that proposes the next few tokens as guesses for the big model to check.
The big, accurate model whose output you actually want. It verifies the draft's guesses and has the final say.
The fraction of drafted tokens the target model keeps. Higher acceptance means a bigger speedup.
The probability rule that decides which drafted tokens to keep, guaranteeing the final output matches the big model's own distribution exactly.
A change in speed with zero change in the result. Speculative decoding is lossless; quantization, by contrast, is lossy.
Extra small layers built into a model that predict several future tokens at once, acting as a free, built-in draft model.
